_comment: Rotating blog idea bank. The Saturday job picks the first idea with used=false, writes the draft, and marks it used. Each run also tops the bank back up with 2 fresh Claude-generated ideas, so it never runs dry. To force a specific topic this week, open a 'Blog idea for Saturday' issue instead; the issue takes priority over this bank.

ideas.title: The meeting that should have been a message
ideas.angle: Most meetings are a tax on focus. When a meeting earns its place, and how to protect deep work without becoming the person who declines everything.

ideas.title: Why I write things down before I am sure
ideas.angle: Writing is not the record of thinking, it is the thinking. How drafting badly on purpose gets you to the real idea faster.

ideas.title: The first 30 days on a new team
ideas.angle: What actually builds trust early is not impressive output, it is reliability on small things. A field guide for the new person.

ideas.title: Saying I do not know in a room full of experts
ideas.angle: The fastest way to lose a team is to bluff. Why admitting the gap out loud is a leadership move, not a weakness.

ideas.title: The to-do list is lying to you
ideas.angle: Long lists feel productive and protect you from doing the one hard thing. Cutting the list down to what actually moves the needle.

ideas.title: Feedback that people can actually use
ideas.angle: Vague praise and vague criticism are both useless. What specific, kind, and timely feedback looks like in practice.

ideas.title: On being interruptible
ideas.angle: There is a cost to always being available and a different cost to never being available. Finding the line as someone people depend on.

ideas.title: The project that taught me to ship smaller
ideas.angle: Big-bang launches hide problems until they are expensive. Why shipping the smallest honest version first beats the grand plan.

ideas.title: Mentoring is not advice
ideas.angle: The best thing you can give someone is not your answer, it is better questions. How to mentor without taking over.

ideas.title: Burnout does not announce itself
ideas.angle: It does not show up as collapse, it shows up as not caring about work you used to love. Noticing it early, in yourself and in others.

ideas.title: The email I almost did not send
ideas.angle: A small story about reaching out to someone above my level, the fear of looking foolish, and what came back.

ideas.title: Tools do not fix culture
ideas.angle: Every new app promises to make the team better. Why the problem is almost never the tool, and what to fix instead.

ideas.title: The Meeting That Could Have Been a Message
ideas.angle: Most meetings solve a coordination problem that a well-written paragraph would handle. What makes a meeting actually worth everyone's time, and how to tell the difference before you send the invite.

ideas.title: Learning in Public
ideas.angle: Sharing work before it is finished feels risky. But the researchers and builders who do it consistently seem to grow faster. What it costs, what it gives back, and how to start small.

ideas.title: When to Stop Asking and Just Decide
ideas.angle: There is a version of collaborative leadership that never actually commits. How to know when you have gathered enough input and it is time to make the call yourself, without apology.

ideas.title: The Email You Should Send Before the Meeting
ideas.angle: Most meetings are bad because everyone arrives cold. One short email sent the day before, with the actual question on the table, changes the whole dynamic. A practical post on how to frame it.
